coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of sulfur-rich nitrogen dots from a single source precursor and its application in dual-mode sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Zhijiao
Yang, Jiani
Li, Gongke
Hu, Yuling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a strategy of sulfur-rich doped nitrogen dots (S-Ndots) based on a single-source precursor (SSP) was developed and applied to sensing of Hg2+. Fluorescent S-Ndots were synthesized by microwave-assisted method using 2-azidothiazole as a single-source precursor, which served as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source simultaneously. This SSP approach guarantee efficient core doping and improve the effect of doping in a molecular level, which is more efficient and simpler than the doping strategy with multiple precursors. The results showed that the as-synthesized S-Ndots have high sulfur component up to 24.6%, and their specific structure effectively promoted artificial peroxidase activity and coordination interaction. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity could be quenched obviously and the peroxidase activity of the S-Ndots were selectively inhibited by Hg2+, then the S-Ndots were applied to the dual-mode sensing of Hg2+ with both fluorometric and colorimetric readout. The S-Ndots-based sensing assay with simplicity and rapidity showed high selectivity to Hg2+ with the detection limit of 0.1 μmol/L by fluorescence spectroscopy and a distinguishable change in the color was observed by the naked eye. In addition, the accuracy and precision were evaluated based on the quantitative detection of Hg2+ in industrial waste water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, the S-Ndots with good biocompatibility were further explored for imaging of Hg2+ in A549 cells. As a novel member of nitrogen quantum dots family, the S-Ndots hold great promise to broaden its applications in environmental and biological sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur-rich doped nitrogen dots
Single-source precursor
Mercury
Dual-mode sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibition of the peroxidase-like activity of the S-Ndots by Hg2+
Cytotoxicity assays and imaging for Hg2+
The design of S-Ndots and its properties
S-Ndots as peroxidase mimetics and colorimetric detection of Hg2+
Dual-mode nanoprobe and detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples
Toxicity study on S-Ndots and cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, Inset: size distribution, (b) AFM image, (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) XPS survey of as-prepared S-Ndots.

(a) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of S-Ndots. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-Ndots in an aqueous solution with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 500 nm in 20 nm increments.

(a) Effect of different metal ions on the FL properties of S-Ndots, including Hg2+, Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and the mix ions under the same conditions. (b) FL Intensity of the S-Ndots on different concentration of Hg2+; and the inset shows the linear dependence of (F0-F)/F0 on Hg2+ concentration from 0.5 to 10 μmol/L.

(a) Effect of metal ions on the artificial peroxidase activity of S-Ndots; (b) Plot of absorbance values versus the concentration of Hg2+. The insets show the linear relationship ranging from 4.0 to 40 μmol/L and the photograph of the catalytic reactions with different concentrations of Hg2+.
Cell viability of A549 cells incubated with the S-Ndots at different concentrations for 24 h and 48 h, respectively.
FL Confocal microscopic images of A549 cells incubated with the S-Ndots for 2 h at 37 °C (a) and further incubated with Hg2+ for another 1 h (b).
Synthesis strategy of the S-Ndots using 2-azidothiazole as a single source precursor.
Recoveries of S-Ndots sensing for Hg2+ in real industrial waste water samples.
Application of S-Ndots sensing for Hg2+ in real industrial waste water samples* .

head.ce:title: Synthesis of sulfur-rich nitrogen dots from a single source precursor and its application in dual-mode sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a strategy of sulfur-rich doped nitrogen dots (S-Ndots) based on a single-source precursor (SSP) was developed and applied to sensing of Hg2+. Fluorescent S-Ndots were synthesized by microwave-assisted method using 2-azidothiazole as a single-source precursor, which served as carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source simultaneously. This SSP approach guarantee efficient core doping and improve the effect of doping in a molecular level, which is more efficient and simpler than the doping strategy with multiple precursors. The results showed that the as-synthesized S-Ndots have high sulfur component up to 24.6%, and their specific structure effectively promoted artificial peroxidase activity and coordination interaction. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity could be quenched obviously and the peroxidase activity of the S-Ndots were selectively inhibited by Hg2+, then the S-Ndots were applied to the dual-mode sensing of Hg2+ with both fluorometric and colorimetric readout. The S-Ndots-based sensing assay with simplicity and rapidity showed high selectivity to Hg2+ with the detection limit of 0.1 μmol/L by fluorescence spectroscopy and a distinguishable change in the color was observed by the naked eye. In addition, the accuracy and precision were evaluated based on the quantitative detection of Hg2+ in industrial waste water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, the S-Ndots with good biocompatibility were further explored for imaging of Hg2+ in A549 cells. As a novel member of nitrogen quantum dots family, the S-Ndots hold great promise to broaden its applications in environmental and biological sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-rich quantum dots (N-dots) have emerged as a new type of quantum dots, which were demonstrated distinct and excellent optical properties [1]. These N-dots contain higher percentage of electron-rich N atoms content compared to most the neighboring carbon dots, which could drastically alter their electronic characteristics and exhibit unique luminescence properties [2,3]. The N-dots have gained tremendous attention because of their high nitrogen component and fascinating properties, which were also further investigated in biocompatible staining, sensing of amino acid, and other applications [1–5]. Tang et al. first reported N-dots as a new type of quantum dots in 2014, which have abundant nitrogen and show unique fluorescence property [1]. In our previous work, fluorescent amino nitrogen quantum dots were synthesized and further explored by combining with AuNPs as the nanoprobe for sensing of cysteine in biological samples [2]. Nevertheless, there are no attempts to explore other heteroatom such as S doped N-dots, and tremendous efforts are still ongoing to explore the full potential of N-dots and its derivatives.

Currently, doping with both sulfur and nitrogen may introduce more active sites and new properties in nanomaterials [6,7], which also show attractive applications based on the synergy effect of the doped sulfur and nitrogen atoms [8–10]. For example, as compared with single-species doping (N or S alone), N,S co-doping significantly improved the electrocatalytic activity towards Cd2+ (>90%) and Pb2+ (>20%) because of the synergistic effects between the dual dopants [6]. Qiao et al. described the first design and preparation of N and S dual-doped mesoporous graphene as a metal-free catalyst for ORR, which shows excellent catalytic activity including a highly positive onset potential and very high kinetic limiting current, which makes it closely comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst [8]. In addition, increasing sulfur-doped content in nitrogen-rich nanomaterials could effectively enhance chemical activity and coordination interaction between S with Hg2+ [11–13], since S can change the electronic structure and provide emissive trap states for photoexcited electron capture [14–16]. Great attention have been devoted to the synthesis of S-doped nanomaterials using cysteine [9], sulfuric acid [17], glutathione [18], polythiophenes [19,20], and thiourea [21] as sulfur sources, but most of the sulfur doping were required extra sulfur-based dopants，and which have relatively less content of element sulfur, lower than 10%. Especially, it is hard to control core doping because mixture of two or more precursors that have different reactivity and structures cannot guarantee efficient doping [22]. In addressing this, it is essential to develop a novel strategy based on a single precursor carrying both core atoms and dopant, which not only guarantee the effect of doping in a molecular level but also ensure efficient doping. To the best of our knowledge, sulfur-rich nitrogen quantum dots have rarely been reported, and their research in unique optoelectronic properties and application are still inchoate. Therefore, the desire for new quantum dots based on abundant sulfur and nitrogen elements is a big challenge.

The mercury ions (Hg2+) as well-known environmental pollutants, exert serious medical effects because of its high toxicity and easy accumulation in the human body [23,24]. Hence, developing a practical available detection gadget or sensor and establishing a facile method of Hg2+ become increasingly requirement. Recently, optical nanosensors for Hg2+ have received great attention due to the advantages of easy operation and fast response [25–27]. Most of previously reported nanosensors have been focused on single readout, either fluorometric or colorimetric mode, which may be difficult to meet the requirements of the accuracy and diverse assay. Fortunately, dual-mode sensing assays provides a promising alternative for more reliable detection because they could offer two kinds of output mode simultaneously, thus providing improved accuracy and attractive diversity [28–30].

Herein, we present a novel strategy to synthesize fluorescent S-Ndots using 2-azidothiazole as a single-source precursor (SSP) by microwave-assisted approach. This SSP approach guarantee efficient doping after self-polymerization, ring-opening and nuclear burst to the forming S-Ndots. The as-prepared S-Ndots with sulfur up to 24.6%, which exhibit excellent peroxidase activity, good biocompatibility and high fluorescence quantum yield, and their specific structure effectively promoted artificial peroxidase activity and coordination interaction with Hg2+. Furthermore, the S-Ndots are found to serve as a novel fluorescent sensing platform for Hg2+ due to the obvious quenching effect of Hg2+. Meanwhile, the resulting S-Ndots catalyze the oxidation of the 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue color product in the presence of hydrogen peroxide, and the peroxidase activity of the S-Ndots was selectively inhibited by Hg2+, which subsequently produced color change. Therefore, a highly sensitive and selective dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorometric) sensor based on the S-Ndots for the detection of Hg2+ was proposed. Moreover, the S-Ndots by the toxicity studies displayed good biocompatibility, which were further used for imaging of Hg2+ in human lung adenocarcinoma (A549) cells with high discrimination.

Quinine sulfate (suitable for fluorescence, 98%) was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-aminothiazole (97%) was obtained from Shanghai Shaoyuan Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aluminium chloride (99.99%), sodium chloride (99.5%), magnesium sulfate (99%), and zinc nitrate (98.0%) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Dichloromethane, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), dichloromethane, acetic acid (HAc), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99.8%), and Hg2+ standard liquid were purchased from J&K (Beijing, China). Bovine serum and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), penicillin-streptomycin, and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). The all chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without any further treatment, from Shenyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Shenyang, China).

1HNMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVB-400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker biospin, Switzerland). Microwave synthesis experiments were performed on a MWave-5000 microwave apparatus (Sineo Microwave Chemistry Technology Company, Shanghai, China). Infrared absorption spectra were recorded with a NICOLET AVATAR 330 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired by a PHILIPS TECNAI 10 TEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded with a Dimension FastScan (Bruker, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained on the UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded with a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Fluorescence imaging of A549 cells was examined by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM 710NLO, Germany). The FL spectra and images of the resulting solutions were recorded at room temperature (25 °C). The absorbance of the cell viability by CCK-8 was measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Multiskan GO, USA).

The raw S-Ndots were prepared in 10 min under 500 W by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using 2-azidothiazole (1.0 g) dissolved in deionized water (100 mL) at 140 °C, and the stock solution finally turned to dark brown, suggested that the raw S-Ndots were synthesized. The supernatant was collected by removing the large dots through filtration with a 0.2 µm filter after centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min. After vacuum-freeze drying, cinnamon-colored solid residue was obtained. The procedures of other quantum dots for the control through 2-aminothiazole as a precursor were similar to the S-Ndots.

In 5 mL colorimetric tubes, successively added 1.0 mL S-Ndots (0.01 mg/mL), and 1.0 mL different concentrations of Hg2+ or sample solution. The mixed solutions were equilibrated for 5 min before spectral measurements. Other metal ions (2 times of the concentration of Hg2+ ions, 40 μmol/L), including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe3+, were added separately to the S-Ndots solution with the same procedures. The excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrophotometer was set as 330 nm, excitation and emission slit widths were both 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of the peroxidase-like activity of the S-Ndots by Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: First, 200 μL of the S-Ndots (0.2 mg/mL) were added into the solution which contains different amounts of Hg2+ (200 μL). After incubation at 37 °C for 5 min, to each centrifuge tube of 2.0 mL, 400 μL of the mixed solution as described above, 400 μL of NaAc-HAc (pH 4.0), 100 μL of TMB (1.0 mmol/L) and 100 μL of H2O2 (5.0 mmol/L) were mixed thoroughly. The mixture was shaken gently and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then the optical intensity was recorded by a UV–Vis 2600 spectrophotometer at 652 nm (A652). To study the selectivity of the inhibition, other metal ions (30 μmol/L) as mentioned above, were added separately to the above catalytic reaction solution and the optical intensity was recorded in the same way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and imaging for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluate the cytotoxicity and demonstrate the imaging in living cells of S-Ndots by a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). In order to survey the intracellular uptake of Hg2+ with S-Ndots through its specific binding, the A549 cells in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS were employed, which were firstly added to confocal plate and then cultured for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). Prior to cell imaging experiments, the A539 cells were washed for three times with PBS buffer and then incubated with the S-Ndots dilute solution (200 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 2 h. The cells were further incubated with extra Hg2+ for another 1 h after the samples were rinsed with PBS buffer to remove the remaining S-Ndots. Finally, the A549 cells mounted with paraformaldehyde on confocal plate for fluorescence imaging by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design of S-Ndots and its properties
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave-assisted strategy significantly decreased the reaction time and enhanced the photoelectric property of quantum dots [37,38]. In this work, the S-Ndots were synthesized by a microwave method with 2-azidothiazole in water, where the formation of nitrogen quantum dots and the sulfur doping were accomplished simultaneously. Compared with most of sulfur doped nanomaterials (several hours or longer), the present SSP strategy via microwave irradiation is more time-saving (10 min). To further optimize the performance of S-Ndots, we have also investigated the influence of microwave irradiation time on fluorescence properties of the resultant S-Ndots. The results showed that the reaction took just 10 min provided S-Ndots exhibiting high fluorescence quantum yield up to 40.1%, which is higher than most of S doped carbon dots prepared with two or much more precursors [13,31,39].

The size and morphology of the obtained S-Ndots were determined by TEM and AFM [2]. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the S-Ndots were well monodispersed and uniform with an average size of 5.0 nm. FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 1 c) further confirm the presence of various functional groups, including C˭O, C˭C, C-N, O-H and C-S. As depicted in Fig. 1 d, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results indicate that the S-Ndots are mainly composed of carbon, sulfur, nitrogen and oxygen. Furthermore, the C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and S 2p spectra were shown in Fig. S6. The C1s analysis revealed five different types of carbon atoms: graphitic(C˭C), aliphatic (C-C), oxygenated (C-O, C˭O, and O-C˭O-), nitrogen-containing (C-N and C˭N) and sulfur-containing (C-S) ( Fig. S6 a). Especially, as shown as elemental analysis results, the obtained S-Ndots have sulfur component up to 24.6%, which is remarkably higher than the S-CDs reported previously ( Table S2 ).

Sulfur doped nitrogen quantum dots with abundant sulfur content promoted coordination interaction, and the binding affinity between Hg2+ and nitrogen or sulfur atoms has been widely reported previously [13,16,41]. Then, it was believed that the proposed S-Ndots with high percentage of N and S content would selectively bind to Hg2+ in aqueous solution, resulting in the change of optical signals in the sensing system. Therefore, the feasibility of using S-Ndots for Hg2+ fluorescence detection was carried out. To evaluate the selectivity of S-Ndots as fluorescent probes, we examined the FL intensity changes in the presence of other metal ions under the same conditions, including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ (
Fig. 3 a). In contrast, no distinct decrease was found on addition of other representative ions into S-Ndots solution. Therefore, the as-synthesized S-Ndots show good selectivity when used as the fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the linear range is obtained from 0.5 to 10.0 μmol/L (R2 =0.9997) and the detection limit is 0.1 μmol/L, which is below the reported previously [13,41]. In addition, it should be noted that the high QY (40.1%) of the S-Ndots is higher than that some previously reported sensor; and it was fascinating especially considering without passivation, favorable for sensitive Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-Ndots as peroxidase mimetics and colorimetric detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the S,N-doped carbon materials show higher catalytic activity compared to the nitrogen-doped carbon materials through a cooperative effect [10,11,42]. Herein, the S-Ndots were found to exhibit intrinsic catalytic activity, which could catalytically oxidize TMB by H2O2 with a blue color reaction. The absorbance of the S-Ndots system at 652 nm, as the typical absorbance peak of oxidation products of TMB [43–45] was much higher than that of the N-dots without sulfur doping, which reflects the intrinsic catalytic activity of S-Ndots as a peroxidase mimic. In addition, the frontier molecular orbital theory was used for further explaining the intrinsic catalytic activity of S-Ndots. The calculated results of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for the S-Ndots and N-dots are presented in Fig. S10, respectively, indicating that sulfur atoms is more effective in reducing the band gap [13,32]. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 package.

Interestingly, from further investigation we found that the addition of Hg2+ would inhibit the catalytic activity of the S-Ndots, and the phenomenon can be applied to the colorimetric sensing and detection of Hg2+ by measuring the decrease of signal. Different concentrations of Hg2+ were incubated with the S-Ndots (0.2 mg/mL) and then the mixtures were applied to the TMB–H2O2 reaction solution. To evaluate the selectivity of the colorimetric assay, for Hg2+, the absorbance of the sensing system was examined in the presence of other metal ions under the same conditions, including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ as mentioned above (
Fig. 4 a). The A652 value as a function of the concentration of Hg2+ was plotted in Fig. 4 b, which decreased gradually as the concentration of Hg2+ increased. A good linear relationship between the Hg2+ concentration ranging from 4.0 to 40 μmol/L and the A652 values (R2 =0.9991) was observed, with the detection limit as 1.0 μmol/L. The S-Ndots-based colorimetric method for the detection of Hg2+ is much more sensitive than other colorimetric methods reported previously [41,46]. Compared with other sensors ( Table S3 ), our method has out-standing advantages including wider linear range, simple preparation procedure and allowable application in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode nanoprobe and detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples
Toxicity study on S-Ndots and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to broaden the application of S-Ndots in biological system, we developed the S-Ndots using fluorescent probe for imaging the intracellular Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the A549 cells show strong bright blue fluorescence after incubation with S-Ndots (0.2 mg/mL) for 2 h, intimating that S-Ndots successfully entered into the A549 cells via endocytosis. Then, the A549 cells were treated with exogenous Hg2+ (20 μmol/L) for another 1 h after removing the remaining S-Ndots with PBS buffer to rinse for three times, where the FL intensity were quenched upon the intracellular Hg2+ ( Fig. 6 b). Notably, the FL images are consistent with the quantitative analysis of the FL intensity, indicating that S-Ndots could be applied for imaging Hg2+ in A549 cells.

In summary, we developed a facile strategy for synthesis of fluorescent S-Ndots by microwave-assisted approach using 2-azidothiazole as the single-source precursor. The results show that the S-Ndots have high sulfur component up to 24.6%, and exhibit good photostability and excellent biocompatibility, which ensured their successful application in Hg2+ ion detection and cell imaging. Interestingly, the specific structure of S-Ndots effectively promoted artificial peroxidase activity and coordination interaction, which were applied to the dual-mode sensing of Hg2+ with both colorimetric and fluorometric readout. And the novel strategy provides a promising alternative for more reliable detection because they could offer two kinds of output mode simultaneously, thus providing improved accuracy and attractive diversity. In addition, the sensing assay featured simplicity, rapidity, and ease of operation without further modification. The accuracy and practicability were evaluated based on the quantitative detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples and imaging of Hg2+ in biological samples with satisfactory results. Therefore, our study provides a new sight for preparation of high nitrogen and sulfur component nanomaterials and broadens their application, and the N-dots research will continue to thrive because of the new opportunities provided by heteroatom doping. Moreover, their superior optical properties and catalysis should enable their use in other applications, which are currently underway in our laboratory.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475153 and 21675178), the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2015A030311020), the Special Funds for Public Welfare Research and Capacity Building in Guangdong Province of China (No. 2015A030401036), and the Guangzhou Science and Technology Program of China (No. 201604020165), respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.11.079.
